title: Exponentially Fast Solution State Preparation for the Heat Equation and its use for Option Pricing
authors: Gumaro Rendon, Stepan Smid, Sarvagya Upadhyay
abstract: In this work, we present the methods necessary to price an important set of derivatives on a quantum device while offering an advantage over existing classical methods. The methods developed here, in conjunction with ~\cite{GumaroS2026}, also provide an exponential advantage in requirement of qubits when pricing some option contracts with path-dependent payoff compared to state-of-the-art quantum Monte Carlo methods.
summary: This work applies advanced quantum computing techniques to the problem of pricing complex financial derivatives. It achieves this by mathematically transforming the governing partial differential equations into a solvable, diagonal heat equation. The resulting quantum algorithm promises an exponential advantage in computational resources over current classical methods for these high-dimensional problems.
main_problem: Developing quantum algorithms to achieve exponential speedups in solving complex Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) that arise from pricing financial derivatives.
main_result: The authors propose methods to solve diffusion-type PDEs, like the heat equation, using quantum techniques that achieve superior scaling compared to classical Monte Carlo or direct PDE solvers.
method: The approach involves transforming the pricing PDE into a time-dependent, diagonal heat equation, which is then solved quantum mechanically via Hamiltonian simulation techniques exploiting fast-forwardable structure.
model_or_system: The underlying model is the pricing of financial derivatives (e.g., swaptions, cross-currency swaptions) whose value functions satisfy parabolic PDEs derived from stochastic differential equations (SDEs) under risk-neutral measures.
key_observables: The option price itself, the solution surface of the PDE, and the required qubit count for computation.
important_parameters: The time horizon $T$, the required precision $\epsilon$, and the dimensionality of the underlying asset space.
main_assumptions: The primary assumptions include the ability to reduce the complex PDE to a heat-type equation, the piecewise-constant nature of volatilities, and the theoretical feasibility of extracting the full solution surface without negating the quantum speedup.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the financial modeling (SDEs $ightarrow$ PDEs via Feynman-Kac) to the mathematical reduction (transforming the PDE into a diagonal heat equation) and finally detailing the quantum algorithmic implementation for solving this reduced form.
why_interesting: The core technique of reducing a complex, high-dimensional PDE to a simple, solvable heat equation structure is mathematically analogous to finding effective Hamiltonians or simplified dynamics in many-body quantum systems, making the quantum simulation aspect highly relevant.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Circuit to implement an m-qubit LCU implementation of e−βA. U is e−iπA/P. The Uprep applied on |0⟩⊗m prepares the square root of the positive-definite coefficients of the LCU.

Figure 3: Solving the heat equation ∂tψ = ∂2 xψ with initial condition ψ0(x) ∝cos(5πx) + 2 cos(πx) + 4 and periodic boundary conditions, comparing the exact solution to the one obtained with FFLCU, using n = 5 qubits for the state, m = 4 qubits for the LCU, and k = 5 ancilla qubits for Grover-Rudolph method used for encoding the LCU coefficients. Here, all functions are al- ways rescaled to have ℓ2 norm equal to 1 (note that this property is normally not preserved during evolution under heat equation).
